speaker: interviewer
text: What's the date today?

speaker: interviewer
text: 2nd of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the 2nd of August. I'm Phyllis Dreazen speaking with Leo Melamed. We're in Chicago. We'll be speaking in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo Melamed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Melamed, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-E-L-A-M-E-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo-- well, Leible Melamdovich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Melamdovich is M-E-L-A-M-D-O-V-I-C-H. Leible is L-E-I-B-L-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No

speaker: interviewer
text: How is your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ben [? Gimpel. ?] No, ben-- ben Yitzhak.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 20, '32.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bialystok, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your earliest memories about Bialystok and your life there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you must remember that I left Bialystok-- I was only seven years old. So that the memories would be ones that were before that. And probably at the age of five that one begins to remember things. I remember that it was a very happy secure life. I lived with my parents, of course, and my grandmother, my maternal grandmother. And I would play mostly with myself, because my parents were teachers. And I was left pretty much to my own devices as a child. And there weren't a lot of little kids in the neighborhood at my age, so that I would end up roaming around the nearby the neighborhood. Not very far, one didn't wander very far at that age, but making up games and living a fairly secure existence as a child. It was happy memories of games and times with my grandmother and eating Bialystok [INAUDIBLE] with herring and-- and the normal life that one would think a child endures up to the age of seven. And of course, then, of course, in my life, everything changed. But up till then, I can't give you any specific memories except the fact that I had very few friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would-- you know, the one friend I remember would make up games with me. He was my age. And he would come over, or I would go to his nearby house. And we would play together, the normal games of kids play.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the games?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the games were the kind of things that you would think. It was making up we were ship captains. Or, we were pirates or the kind of things like marbles that was played then, rolling a ball into a little hole in the ground that you had to try and execute, those kinds of games that I do remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember that pretty well. It was a frame-- a wooden frame bungalow. It had a bedroom. My-- my bed was a crib in the early days. Later on, I got a cot. We had a living room that was both a living room and a dining room. It had a day bed in it where guests sometimes would sleep. The living room-- the feature of the living room besides the table was a wall of white brick oven that was used both for heating as well as for cooking. My grandmother would cook cholent in the oven. And there was a kitchen. The kitchen had a potbelly stove in it. It was black, and it was short. And it was squat. And there was a wash room off the kitchen. An indoor plumbing facility was rare, especially in the neighborhood we lived. But my father was very handy. And he had put that together. And he even changed it from the plumbing to the new modern plumbing, which was an overhead box. And he installed that himself. And I recall helping him during that process, handing him tools and things like that. Then there was a small yard in the front. And there was a staircase. The upstairs would lead to the attic. There, my grandmother had her room where she slept. And the downstairs would lead to a basement. It's very small. But always, as I remember, it was cold in there. And, of course, that was used for preserving whatever it was that my grandmother would preserve in the big jars that she would keep there, whether it was compote of some sort or sauerkraut or things like that pickles. And I would very seldom go in there. But-- and even less often, would go visit upstairs to my grandmother's room. That's the extent of the-- of the house. It wasn't very big. But it-- it-- it was considered quite well-off for what we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the neighborhood. Was it a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish neighborhood, but not exclusively so. [DRINKS] There were non-Jews in the neighborhood. But mostly, it was predominantly Jews. And, in fact, and-- and not very far, a couple of houses down across the street lived a relative of ours. His name was-- I think it was Maier. And he was an egg merchant, which I thought in later years when I became an egg trader at the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, I thought it was kind of funny. Because I was carrying forward the tradition in the family. I once talked to my father about that. He laughed. He was a real merchant in terms of real eggs. I was just trading futures. But so they lived nearby. And on occasion, I would visit their house, too. I don't recall much about their house except that there was a very large table. And my-- whoever was the relative would sit me down to give me some food as soon as I entered the-- her premises. The other feature that I do remember is that at the far end of the neighborhood was a small synagogue where I would only visit on rare occasions. Because my parents were not the religious type. So I don't think we were members of the synagogue. So the only way I would ever come there is through-- when some friends would take me with them or something like that. I don't think I entered more than once or twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your impressions of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was kind of a feeling of respect that I didn't fully understand, because of the quiet nature of the-- of the inner feel for the place. But I didn't come there to pray. And I wasn't taught anything about prayers. Of course, I was a little young anyway at-- my memories are. But I do remember that on holiday there would be a-- a milling of people around the small synagogue. And I would sometimes watch them from afar. They would come with their [INAUDIBLE], of course. Everybody that was religious, there wasn't anything but orthodox religion. There weren't any other forms. So everybody would come with their [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]. And I would watch with respect, but from a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your neighborhood, did the religious and the non-religious, which is to say your parents and the other parents, did they get along? Did they make friendships?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no memory of any interaction or acrimony between them. So I was too young. I was fairly well-shielded from those kinds of issues. It wasn't until war broke out that I encountered the issue of religion and my father's beliefs on a different level. But the memories you're talking about or you're asking me about, I don't have any real instances to give you any examples about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a bit about your parents and your grandmother and, if you like, their names for the testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandmother was Itke, and-- Itke Barakin, which was my mother's maiden name. My mother is Chaya Fejgla Barakin. But she was officially Fejgla Barakin. And my father, of course, was Isaac Moshe. And my mother would call him 'May-she' Melamdovich. The most-- obviously, your parents are very important to you as a child. But my grandmother took almost precedent in terms of importance, because I was with her maybe more often than with my parents. Because at that age, my parents worked. And I would be left at home with my grandmother. So then she was always there. My parents were only there after school. They were teachers. And they would come home late in the evening, 5:00 or 6:00-ish for dinner. So that-- and they were gone most of the day, from the morning on. So that I was always with-- in the company of my grandmother or my father's sister, who was a young lady probably in her 20s, early 20s. And my memory of her is that she was the most beautiful girl I ever met. That may be a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she had dark hair. And she was very-- good figure and always wore a white blouse and a blue skirt or a blue dress as I remember, you know. And she was a picture of beauty to me, you know. I was very infatuated with her. She was my friend and also sort of a mentor and the only adult that I considered more or less on my level. She took to me that way. We would go for walks and conversation and that kind of thing. And I also remember her coming along sometimes when we would go for summer holiday, because my parents being teachers were free during the summer vacation months. And we would always go to a dacha, somewhere probably [INAUDIBLE] on the-- on the [INAUDIBLE] river or some other rivers that I don't remember, where there would be pine forests. And we would go for Bobble, was her name, the-- my aunt, my father's sister. And Bobble and I would go for very long walks in the-- in the forest. These were very, very tall oak trees and pine trees. And it was a real comfortable feeling of having her hold my hand as we walked and talked. Children talk, I'm sure, because she was probably accommodating me during this-- these kinds of interludes. But I remember them. They stand out in my mind. And so I really loved her and my grandmother, who was my safety valve, my grandmother would give me breakfast and lunch and later on, of course, dinner when my parents would come back to the house. And we would have dinner in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you call her bubbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bubbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the best thing she cooked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I recall, of course, the cholent, which was a favorite of mine. And that would be a stew that would have to sit in the oven for 24 hours without stirring. And it had all kinds of ingredients in it that was very good to my taste anyway. But the thing that I loved the most was even more than the cholent, which would be a daily food herring. I-- I enjoyed herring with the Bialystok kuchen as I mentioned. And my grandmother knew that for me the tail of the herring was the most important part. And she would give me that, because I wouldn't have to hold a fork. You could hold the tail and eat it by holding the tail, rather than a piece in the middle which you needed a fork to eat the herring with. So that's how she'd give it to me. And then, of course, the Bialystok kuchen, which became world famous-- you know, that's where the word bialy comes from-- would be my favorite meal with the herring. That's what my grandmother gave me. The other meals-- I was a pretty bad eater.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was almost always a fight to feed me. I was very thin. And my mother was always worried about my weight. And they used to try and spoon feed me with all kinds of porridge and things I didn't like, chicken soup, for instance. I'm one of the few Jews that in my adulthood don't eat chicken soup, because of the force feeding that I encountered as a child I think. So I rejected it when I finally left my home. Similarly, with rice, I have this problem with rice. And it must be from this force feeding that I was subjected to as a child. No matter where, my parents and my bubbe and everybody would try to get me to eat more. I seem to have learned how to eat after I left the house anyway. You mentioned that your house was not a religious household. But how did you notice, if not celebrate, Shabbas and the various [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, there's always a holiday feeling around Shabbas, because my parents work. And so it was different. You celebrated, because there was-- there was a feeling of a day of rest. And although, it wasn't in religious terms, we didn't say prayers and go to the synagogue, but there were nevertheless was a feeling. The meal was different. The fact my parents were there was quite different. We ate together during the day, which is different. So there was that feeling. And, again, I was very young during that period of time. So that I may-- may have not known other rituals that occurred. But my grandmother did light candles. So there was always candles lit, because my grandmother was religious. And she would light the candles. And they would, of course, be lit during the Sabbath. So I knew it was a holiday. I knew it was something special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any friction between your grandmother and her son about religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her-- her daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Not that I know. If there was, they kept it from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the celebration of any holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, celebration of holidays was a little different. Because my parents always had a curriculum activity that related to the holidays. I mean, if it was-- certainly if it was Passover, there were special events that occurred at the house as well. I don't remember precisely what they were. But I knew that the meal was different and-- and that there was a story of Passover about. But my parents would also celebrate those holidays in a different fashion with their school children. And I was the beneficiary of that, so that I always knew that my mother or father told their classes about the pharaoh and the Moses and how the Jews left the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, and traveled across the desert. I knew those stories simply by having heard them that my parents would sometimes-- my mother would sometimes take me along to her classroom during that period of time. And I would be able to hear her tell the students. And in her case, the students were weren't older students. She had younger class-- classes. So that these were kids not that much older I was. If I was six, maybe they were eight or nine. And so that I really-- I-- I-- there was a camaraderie that I could understand. And that's how holidays are recorded in my mind at least in the early days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your parents teach? And what did they teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Bialystok in Poland had its own Yiddish schools as did our Warszawa, which is Warsaw, Lodz, and VIlna. I don't know of any other Polish cities were allowed to have full-time parochial schools, meaning in Yiddish where the full curriculum was in Yiddish. And you graduated from there to go on to gymnasium, which is high school. And it was a Polish high school. So it was accredited grammar schools from which you could go to high school, and then later if you could get to university. Of course, Jews didn't get to university very often. But the schools were accredited, and therefore needed qualified teachers to be accredited. My mother, for instance, had become a teacher in the Vilna teacher's seminar, which was very famous throughout Poland as a very high level of school of education for teachers. My father didn't. He-- he went to-- I think it was a Belgian school. I forgot the name right here now of where he graduated. But at any rate, my mother went to Vilna seminar. And my father must have attended classes there as well. Although, he wasn't a full-time student. Because I never saw his graduation papers. I saw hers. My mother was a straight-A student. So she a couple of times showed me her graduation and her marks. But my father met my mother at the Vilna seminar. So I know that he must have attended some classes there. At any rate, they were very qualified teachers. And my mother taught the younger-- now, in-- in that day when you taught a class, you taught them everything. There wasn't any you-- you went to a science teacher for science or a math teacher for math. The grade school teacher was your teacher in every subject. And the curriculum was complete until you were moved onto the next grade, and then you got another teacher that had its curriculum for the following grade. My mother had the younger grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she would have first grade, second grade, and third grade. Those were her classes. My father, on the other hand, who was a mathematician whose books were used throughout Poland, Yiddish schools-- the math books that he wrote for grade school students eight nine, eighth and ninth grade, seventh, eighth, and ninth grades, were used throughout Poland in Yiddish schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he, of course, taught-- taught the older grades which included the higher grade math. But, again, when you were in seventh or eighth grade, your teacher did it all. Whether it was biology or whether it was history or-- or literature or math, it was that teacher that gave it all. You didn't go from classroom to classroom. As I recall, that's how those classes worked. The school they taught in was called [INAUDIBLE], named after its founder, someone named Grosse. I don't know his first name at this time. Oh, my father graduated from the Leipzig Institute as I recall now. And he, too, had very high grades. I remember seeing his [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. So they taught in [INAUDIBLE]. And, of course, it was-- it was a grammar school that began late August, as I recall, and lasted through June. It was a long-- a long good teaching year.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were ready for school, did you go to the same school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [DRINKS] Much to my disappointment, I thought that by-- by virtue of the fact that my parents were teachers, I would be in my mother's classroom. But I was disappointed. Because my mother thought that-- and both my father and mother thought that that wasn't wise, that I would be looked on as a teacher's pet. And it would not do me well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for my benefit, they were going to enroll me and, in fact, brought me to be enrolled in another school in kindergarten. And, of course, war broke out right after that. And so I never really attended first grade.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was going to go to another school, also a Yiddish school, but not their [INAUDIBLE]. And I don't remember the school in which I eventually was enrolled in for kindergarten. Unfortunately, the war interfered anyway. And I never got to be in that school for very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: The language of your home in your youth was Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. Although, I spoke fluently Polish as well. Because Polish was all around us. And you couldn't very well as a child not learn both languages. So Yiddish was spoken in the home and Polish outside the home with non-Jews, which I could speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents involved in community life in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very much so. But the community life was all mostly Jewish oriented. Except that in their case, they were also Bundisten. The Bund was the socialist organization of-- for-- for Jews, and a very active, very successful socialist Bundist organization in Bialystok and throughout Poland. We're talking many hundreds of thousands of numbers, perhaps millions of members throughout Poland of Jews. And my father was a leader in that movement. And my mother, too. I don't know if she was a leader, but certainly an avid member, an enthusiastic member. And so that was a primary cultural activity of my parents. But beyond that, they had school activities and the kind of things that cultural oriented people would have, whether it's theater or reading circles. That was certainly part of their life. But the mainstay was the socialist movement. Because in Poland, that was beyond merely political. It was also cultural. The socialist movement, the Bundist socialist movement in Poland, was a movement that proclaimed equality for Jews on every level wherever they lived. In fact, they rejected Zionism. They rejected Palestine as-- as the end all home for Jews. They felt very strongly that Jews were entitled to live as Jews anywhere in the world and as citizens of that culture of that country that they lived. Never mind whether they were religious. Never mind whether they were not. They were secular Jews or religious Jews. They were citizens of whatever country, and had the same rights as Jews and didn't have to wait until Palestine to gain those rights. So it was really a very, very strong oriented Jew-ish movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any interaction with the local government, the Bialystok city council or anything of that sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- yes, they did. Obviously, this movement was had its impact on the politics of Poland. And there were marches. There were-- there were debates. There was a great deal of political movement. My father was elected as a consequence to the city council of Bialystok. He was one of the few Jews-- and I don't know if whether there were more than two Jewish members of the city council. Obviously, the mayor was not Jewish. But the councilman were mostly non-Jews. And my father was elected, because of his activities in the Bund, Bundist movement of Bialystok. So yes, there was an interaction, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've You've talked very eloquently about the security and happiness of your early life. When did you notice that things didn't feel quite so secure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wasn't even any doubt, notice. I mean, I was advised in no uncertain terms by my father who sat me down to explain that there might be a war. My father was one of those people that believe that little children ought to know everything that is going on around them irrespective of their age. And he would very carefully explain all events going on in the world to me. So I was conscious of things that maybe other kids my age weren't. And he sat me down to explain that there was going to be turmoil, that the-- the chances were that the Germans would invade Poland, and that there would be or could be a war. I didn't understand what war meant, mind you. But I certainly was advised that things were going to be different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning before it happened, I didn't have any feeling about it except that I thought there would be sword fights in the streets. And I really thought the sword fights would be on the big streets, not on the street where we lived. So I would really have to go and watch them to see who was winning. But certainly, I didn't think there was an impact on me personally.
